item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not even remotely close. 4197's arc is wildly different, far more character-based.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not seeing the relation myself, aside from both being related to medicine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted. Just a robotic version of SCP-4197. In fact the test logs are styled the exact same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
" Patient died on operating table. :( "
Oh man that got me.

I really enjoyed this, although if I could make a couple of recommendations:

- maybe make the first test after the dummy a D-class? I was pretty shocked to see them skip right to agents given it's something picked up via MC&D. I know a few tests were skipped so it could have been done in there, but that strikes me as an important benchmark. We wouldn't even have to intentionally mangle a D class, they're probably more frequently in need of surgery than agents by nature.

- it becomes pretty clear early on that it's developing emotions: maybe we could include how it's co-surgeons feel about *it* (or its work), especially the director beyond just a recommendation that it be allowed to work independently.

- I know you probably want to retain the emotional punch of it shutting down forever, but I do think that the foundation would probably have a contingency in case it does ever boot back up. Maybe a suggestion of therapy with a counselor specializing in sentient SCPs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good robot
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can understand calling it shallow, but it's also well-executed. Short, but efficient, and pretty well targeted at my emotional weak points tbh. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Patient died on operating table. :(

nnooOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO

I'm a sucker for a good ole robot with feelings. This was written very concisely and it still managed to pluck at the heartstrings. a solid +1

item.title: Site Criticism Team Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
This comment is a violation of the site rules, principally:

Rule Zero: Don't be a dick. Every other rule follows from this.

Continuing to interact with the site in this manner may result in further disciplinary action. Please be more considerate of the people you interact with.

This is a CLOSED staff post. Do not respond to it unless you are staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ThIs NEeDs mORe ChArAcTer DeveLOpmeNt

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, thanks for replying my comment. And by no means I think my ramblings have to be the final say on anything.

I just reread the article, paid attention, and keep my opinions:

1- Show, don't tell. The article doesn't show any reason to think the current state won't change. If the last shut down included some hardware destruction and was followed by examination, maybe. But simply shutting down isn't a guarantee that an anomalous computer is out for good;

2- Indeed, but see above. Standard containment is enough only after the end of the anomalous properties is beyond doubt, given the risk of an anomalous reactivation causing a breach;

3- Yes, and yet it doesn't change that it sounds like a Task Force report to me and would be better put as such or be removed;

4- It is not only two details, it is the entirety of the second paragraph in the description and the way the first paragraph starts. The first paragraph makes it seems like being from AR is of more relevance than it being anomalous. This is an important info for the investigation, but this is not an investigation report. The second paragraph gives a lot of context and detail, to the extend of even revealing its initial auction price;

5- Missed this, sorry.

My problem with all the context given is that it takes a lot of space, but have no bearing on the core of the article. And no, even it being from AR isn't important, it is *interesting*, but not important.

From an In-Character perspective an SCP article is mainly about the object being studied, so the focus of the text matters. From an OoC perspective it sounds like those details will have some relevance when they won't, at the same time that a proper description of effects and tests is lacking. It is a bad frame for the core concept.

But again, this is my impression and criticism. You only have to take from it what makes sense for you. You can argue otherwise, I'm just saying up front that I write a lot and that can seem to be contentious, it seems. And I do over-explain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. No, the article explicitly makes a big deal about how it won't turn on anymore despite best Foundation efforts.
2. It won't turn on; there's no reason to waste security resources on it when you can shove the broken robot into a box.
3. YMMV
4. Like…. two details? Anderson making it (which is arguably a necessary detail) and MC&D's auction? That's really not a lot.
5. It literally says "Testing logs removed for brevity" after the training dummy session.

I feel that most of your complaints stem from not reading the article closely enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, skips like these get me everytime, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Their critique is literally that this article needed more character development and personality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww, I wish I could hug the robot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you suggesting none of the SCPs should have any actual character development or personalities?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote for me, sorry.

The concept is good and its core, the logs, was well done. As a general narrative I have no qualms.

But I think a lot of aspects from the Foundation got lost for no reason:

1- This is not Neutralized, since it still works, it is just depressed;
2- It shouldn't be in a Standard Containment, first because it is an SCP, secondly because it is Sentient and self-motile;
3- The description cuts right to "it Anderson's, so that's why it is anomalous", giving unnecessary context without describing it or it properties in any relevant way. It sounds like a Task Force report, not the finished article;
4- A lot of details on GOIs' activities that have no bearing in the story and I don't really care;
5- They do no test whatsoever, then someone wants to use it because why not.

Let me be clear, I have nothing against the Foundation deciding to use an anomaly whose effects are actually useful and understood enough for some safety and reliability. But without tests there is no safety and reliability in it. This thing is a frigging autonomous AI, it could do horrible shit out of the blue for unknown reasons, at the very least, without some tests.

Some logs could be those tests, by the way, at least some of them. A single dummy test isn't nearly enough, and even downplaying the dumb "lets fry D-Classes for cheap data"a surgeon just begs for tests with D-Classes that needed the surgery because of accidents and stuff.

As a general concept we already have SCP-2295 and, with a different area of work, SCP-1230. So the good parts aren't enough for not downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: feels good to feel bad sometimes. +1

item.title: Re: A little sad
item.content:encoded: Hah, I keep being unsure if people actually caught that detail. Glad to know at least one person did!

item.title: Re: What makes it an SCP
item.content:encoded: Fair - i wanted to go for a more understated anomalous effect. A lot of Anderson Robotics articles have items that aren't explicitly magical so much as they are tech anomalies. So like, a fully sapient AI that doesn't seem possible with the technology of now seems like it would count as anomalous, since its practically impossible.

item.title: Re: A little sad
item.content:encoded: Note the name of the patient it failed to save- a human surgeon might want to hang up their scrubs after that, too.

item.title: Re: What makes it an SCP
item.content:encoded:
It's a sapient AI that fits in a surgical robot and is good at what it does.

It's also specifically one from Anderson Robotics, a GOI which makes up for the limitations of modern hardware with literal magic.

item.title: What makes it an SCP
item.content:encoded: I like it, it was cute. What makes it an SCP though? Did the foundation just take it so they could save people's lives? I did not catch anomalous properties. That being said I enjoyed this article, this simplistic, shallow writing design is really underrated. I won't be giving a rating simply because I did not notice any anomalous properties and would consider this more of a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See "Good co-surgeon."

Eyes flick down to "Proud."

You're gonna make me cry about a robot, aren't you?

Get to ":(".

Now I'm fucking crying about a robot, thanks ;_; +1

item.title: A little sad
item.content:encoded: I wish the robot didn't shut itself off after one failure. Real doctors fail sometimes, and they still continue after.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't sure if I was gonna upvote, but then I noticed it was at +68, and I knew my hands were tied
It becomes a lot darker once you understand what the title means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short and simple, but well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s competently written, but to me, this reads almost beat for beat as SCP-2295, and I think Patchwork did a better job of it. I’m a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A simple story, but one told well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is cute, i like it +1

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is by the US Army, under Public Domain.

I liked this when I saw it in crit and I’m glad it’s up (feels like forever has gone by, but then this contest has lasted a looooong time)

I don’t think it’s shallow, the implications are quite deep. I liked the withdrawn view we had of the AI, having to read between the lines.

And in the end, I think it’s compelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solid work. Always happy to see people branching out with AR.

While I wish there was little more development on the character of the AI (maybe one or two more logs to really cement things), you still built enough personality and motivations there for me to feel for the poor thing in the end.

+1. Well done, RH.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made me feel sympathy for a fictional AI. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really great seeing this out of the draft. Did you also chance the font of the voice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I'm okay with a shallow piece.

A problem I have with the current trend of SCPs is that there's not too much room for outside interpretation. Series I, while lacking much of the emotional depth and narrative complexity that's par-for-the-course nowadays, was good in that the simple concepts could allow really anyone to engage with it. This one has… room to breathe, if you will.

However, I would have liked a little more interaction between Brey and the bot, and the characterization of the bot seemed a tad too shallow, But overall, the story is nice and concise.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I remember critting this one!

Anyway, happy to see it up. Its simplicity feels like a genuine breath of fresh air after reading all of the complex narratives written for cancon.

Nice job as always, Rounder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Emotional depth feels a bit shallow for me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: a little cheap, a little shallow. reserved.
